coredata.dc:title: Carbon-quantum dots derived from denatured milk for efficient chromium-ion sensing and supercapacitor applications
coredata.dc:creator: Athika, Mattath
Prasath, Arul
Duraisamy, Ezhumalai
Sankar Devi, Vaithiyanathan
Selva Sharma, Arumugam
Elumalai, Perumal
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate here a sustainable approach to generate carbon-quantum dot (CQD) using denatured (spoiled) milk by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The unique photophysical property of the CQD has resulted in selective sensing of the chromium ions (Cr6+) as low as 14 µM in control and environmental water samples. The CQD electrode delivered a specific capacitance of 95 F g−1 with an exceptional stability over 1000 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-quantum Dot
Waste milk
Fluorescent probe
Chromium (VI)
Supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption profile and (b) emission profiles at different excitation wavelengths (λexcitation = 300–430 nm) recorded for the CQD. Inset: Photographs of the CQD under UV and visible light irradiation. (c) XRD pattern, (d) FT-IR spectrum and (e) HR-TEM micrographs recorded for the lyophilized CQD. (Inset: histogram of the CQD grains).

(a) Emission profiles of the CQD probe in the presence of increasing concentrations of the Cr6+: (1) 0, (2) 5, (3) 10, (4) 15, (5) 20, (6) 25, (7) 30, (8) 35, (9) 40, (10) 45, (11), 50 (12) 55, (13) 60, (14) 65, (15) 70, (16) 75, (17) 80, (18) 85, (19) 90.00, (20) 95.00 and (21) 100 µM, (b) The calibration curve for the CQD probe in the presence of varying concentrations of the Cr6+, (c) Time resolved fluorescence spectral profiles of the CQD probe in the absence and presence of Cr6+, (d) Relative fluorescence intensity of the CQD probe in the presence of common interfering metal ions.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms at different scan rates, (b) Galvanostatic charge–discharge profiles at different current densities, (c) Dependence of specific capacitance on the current density, and (d) Cycle-life data along with the Columbic efficiency at 0.12 Ag−1 obtained for the CQD electrode in 1 M H2SO4.

head.ce:title: Carbon-quantum dots derived from denatured milk for efficient chromium-ion sensing and supercapacitor applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrochemical Energy and Sensors Lab, Department of Green Energy Technology, Madanjeet School of Green Energy Technologies, Pondicherry University, Puducherry 605014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Madanjeet School of Green Energy Technologies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate here a sustainable approach to generate carbon-quantum dot (CQD) using denatured (spoiled) milk by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The unique photophysical property of the CQD has resulted in selective sensing of the chromium ions (Cr6+) as low as 14 µM in control and environmental water samples. The CQD electrode delivered a specific capacitance of 95 F g−1 with an exceptional stability over 1000 cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: CQD with their exceptional optical and electronic properties are increasingly used in various applications such as optoelectronics, sensors etc [1]. Of late, the CQD are also emerged as a perspective electroactive material for energy storage applications [2,3]. Green synthesis approach using the natural feedstocks holds great promise for large scale synthesis and multifunctional applications of CQD [4]. In the present work, denatured sour milk was utilized to generate CQD by simple one-pot hydrothermal method. The prepared CQD were explored for multifunctional applications such as energy storage material in supercapacitor and fluorescent probe for selective detection of Cr6+. The detailed preparation and experimental procedure for fluorescence/supercapacitor analysis has been given in the supporting information.

The aqueous dispersion of CQD appeared light yellow under visible light and upon its exposure to ultra violet light (360 nm), a deep blue colour is generated, indicating that the prepared CQD exhibits blue fluorescence. Fig. 1 a and b shows the absorption and emission profiles recorded for the CQD. It is obvious that the absorption profile of the CQD exhibits a narrow band at 290 nm which is attributed to the presence of aromatic π–π* transition [5]. The fluorescent property of the CQD is ascribed to band gap transitions corresponding to conjugated π-domain and surface defects present in the quantum dots. As shown in Fig. S2 that with the increase of the excitation wavelength from 300 to 430 nm, the emission intensity of the CQD showed a gradual increase upto the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, beyond which the CQD showed a drastic decrease in the emission intensity. The maximum emission wavelength of the CQD showed a continuous red shift with respect to the excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. S3, zeta potential of the CQD exhibited a value of −3.87 mV which indicates the presence of negatively charged functional groups on the surface of the CQD.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CQD shown in Fig. 1c reveals distinct Bragg peaks located at diffraction angles, (2θ) 28.48, 40.62, 50.34, 58.72° corresponding to the (0 0 2), (1 0 0), (1 0 2), and (1 0 3) crystal planes of the carbon as per the JCPDS # 98-005-4232 [6]. Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the CQD displayed in Fig. 1d reveals the presence of strong band at 3301 cm−1 which is assigned to OH stretching vibration of hydroxyl group. A weak band observed at 2956 cm−1 is assigned to CH stretching vibration. The peak at 1680 cm−1 is due to the presence of CC stretching vibration. Low intensity bands at 1400 and 1112 cm−1 are attributed to the CN and CNC stretching vibrations, respectively [5,7]. High resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) micrographs revealed that the CQD is well characterized by ultrafine quasi spherical grains with an average grain size of 2 nm (Fig. 1e).

The fluorescent detection of the Cr6+ was carried out by monitoring the emission profiles of the CQD with increasing concentrations of the Cr6+. It is seen from Fig. 2 a that in the absence of the Cr6+, the CQD exhibits an emission maxima at 425 nm upon excitation at 350 nm. Upon the addition each of increasing concentration of the Cr6+, the emission intensity of the CQD resulted in a progressive decrease. As depicted in Fig. S4, the absorption profile of the Cr6+ is characterized by a broad absorption bands at 258 and 352 nm, and a weak band at 440 nm [8]. It is noted that the CQD exhibits a narrow absorption peak at 290 nm and a strong excitation dependent emission maxima in the region of 400–500 nm (Fig. 1b) [8]. This clearly translates a significant overlap of the absorption and emission wavelengths of both the quencher (Cr6+) and the fluorophore (CQD). Thus, it can be inferred that the observed florescence quenching of the CQD seem mainly due to the inner filter effect (IFE) resulting from the shielding of the excitation and emission intensities of the CQD by the added Cr6+. The calibration plot shown in Fig. 2b affirms that the emission spectral responses of the CQD were found to vary linearly with the concentration of the Cr6+ with excellent correlation coefficient of 0.99416. The calculated limit of detection for the Cr6+ in the present work was as low as 14 µM which is comparable or much lower than those reported by other methods (Table S1).

To affirm the IFE quenching mechanism of the CQD in the presence of Cr6+, excited-lifetime measurements were performed. Fig. 2c. shows the fluorescence decay profile of the CQD in the absence and presence of the Cr6+. It is apparent that the fluorescence decay profile of the CQD is a bi-exponential with an average life time of 7.92 ns. The life-time value of the CQD in the presence of the Cr6+ displayed an average life time value of 7.51 ns with no obvious change, which clearly affirms that the fluorescence quenching is driven by IFE rather than by dynamic collision quenching process which involves a decrease in the excited state lifetime [9].

The selectivity of the CQD fluorescence probe to the Cr6+ was investigated in the presence of common interfering metal ions at 50 fold higher concentration of each interfering ions. It is evident from Fig. 2d that the common interfering metal ions did not evoke any significant alteration to the relative fluorescence intensity of the CQD at 425 nm and the significant quenching of the fluorescent CQD probe is closely associated with the addition of the Cr6+. This evidently demonstrates that the present CQD-based fluorescence sensor exhibits excellent selectivity to the Cr6+. To validate the applicability of the present CQD-based florescence probe for detection of the Cr6+, a known concentration of Cr6+ was prepared and spiked into the drinking water. Then, the Cr6+ spiked drinking water was added to the CQD fluorescence probe and its fluorescence intensity was measured. From the calculated value, it is seen that the IFE-based fluorescence sensing strategy showed significant recovery with appreciable sensitivity and accuracy (Table S2).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed to analyze the electrochemical performance of the CQD. Fig. 3 a shows the CVs of the CQD electrode recorded in 1 M H2SO4 in the potential range of 0–0.8 V vs. SCE at various scan rates. It is seen from Fig. 3a that the CV curves of the CQD electrode has a characteristic rectangular shape which clearly implies the existence of double-layer capacitance [10]. The CV current exhibited gradual increase with the increase of scan rate. The outstanding electrochemical stability as well as ideal capacitive characteristics of the electrode material is evident from the unflustered rectangular shaped CV curves even at higher scan rates. Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves were recorded at different current densities. It is evident from Fig. 3b that the charge/discharge curves are almost symmetrical in all currents, indicating the presence of perfect double layer capacitance with nearly 100% Columbic efficiency. Fig. 3c depicts the estimated specific capacitances of the CQD. It is seen that at a current of 0.12 A g−1, the CQD electrode displays a specific capacitance of 95 F g−1. It is seen that the specific capacity value presented here is better or comparative to the values reported elsewhere (Table S3).

The stability of the fabricated CQD electrode material was tested for several charge/discharge cycles at a current density of 0.12 A g−1. The obtained data are displayed in Fig. 3d. It is seen that the CQD electrode exhibits an initial capacity of 40 F g−1 and at the end of 1000th cycle, the capacitance was still retained at 40 F g−1. Moreover, there is no obvious change in the capacitive behaviour was observed during the charging and discharging process even at 1000th cycle, demonstrating excellent long-term cycling stability. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz-100 kHz. It is evident from Fig. S5 that the Nyquist plot consists of a spike (Warburg element) nearly 45° inclined to Y-axis which is indicative of diffusion controlled process [11]. It can be seen that significantly low resistance has been observed for the CQD electrode. Such a low resistance has led to excellent Columbic efficiency over 1000 charge/discharge cycles.

In summary, we have employed waste milk derived CQD as sensitive fluorescence probe for Cr6+ with excellent selectivity and sensitivity. The CQD electrode exhibited a substantial specific capacitance of 95 F g−1 with an exceptional stability as well as Columbic efficiency over 1000 cycles. The waste milk can be a sustainable source for generation of CQD-based nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: MA thanks the CSIR-UGC for JRF (Grant No: 111424), PE thanks SERB for financial support (EMR/2016/001305) and AS thanks SERB for NPDF (PDF/2016/002815).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2019.01.064.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
